1.1.title: IP Routing Concepts
paragraphs: Routers make forwarding decisions one packet at a time. When a packet arrives, the router strips the Layer 2 header, looks up the destination IP address in its routing table, and re-encapsulates the packet for the outgoing interface. Every hop is a fresh decision.

The routing table is keyed by network prefix, not by individual host. When two prefixes both match a destination, the router chooses the one with the longest prefix length — the more specific route wins. This rule, called longest-prefix match, is the single most important concept in IP routing.

1.1.title: show ip route — reading the table
paragraphs: Each line of show ip route output begins with a one-letter code identifying the source: C for connected, S for static, O for OSPF, D for EIGRP, B for BGP, and L for the local /32 host route. The asterisk after a code marks the gateway of last resort.

Bracketed pairs like [110/2] read as [administrative distance / metric]. Administrative distance is the trustworthiness of the source — connected (0) and static (1) outrank dynamic protocols. The metric is the protocol-specific cost used to break ties within a single source.

1.1.title: Longest-Prefix Match in Practice
paragraphs: Suppose a router has both 192.168.0.0/16 and 192.168.5.0/24 in its table. A packet destined to 192.168.5.7 matches both prefixes. The /24 wins because 24 is greater than 16 — it describes a more specific network and is therefore a more confident answer about where the destination lives.

Longest-prefix match is evaluated against the entire routing information base, not just one protocol's contribution. A static /32 host route will beat an OSPF /24 even though OSPF has a richer cost model — specificity is decided before administrative distance.
